class Solution {
public:
    int maxScore(vector<int>& nums) {
        int n = nums.size() / 2;
        unordered_map<int, int> memo;
        function<int(int)> dp;
        dp = [&](int mask) -> int {
            if (mask == (1 << (2*n)) - 1) return 0;
            auto it = memo.find(mask);
            if (it != memo.end()) return it->second;
            // count the number of set bits (used elements)
            int used = 0;
            for (int i=0; i < 2*n; ++i) {
                if (mask & (1 << i)) ++used;
            }
            int op = (used / 2) + 1;
            vector<int> available;
            for (int i=0; i < 2*n; ++i) {
                if ( (mask & (1 << i)) == 0 ) {
                    available.push_back(i);
                }
            }
            int max_score = 0;
            for (int i=0; i < available.size(); ++i) {
                for (int j=i+1; j < available.size(); ++j) {
                    int a = available[i];
                    int b = available[j];
                    int new_mask = mask | (1 << a) | (1 << b);
                    int current_gcd = gcd(nums[a], nums[b]);
                    int current_score = op * current_gcd + dp(new_mask);
                    max_score = max(max_score, current_score);
                }
            }
            memo[mask] = max_score;
            return max_score;
        };
        return dp(0);
    }
};

For each pair, compute the score and then proceed. The optimal choice is to pick (3,6) with gcd 3 for first operation (1*3=3) and then (4,8) with gcd 4 for second operation (2*4=8). Total 11. The available elements are 1 and 3 (values 4 and 8). Their gcd is 4. So 2*4=8. Total is 3+8=11. Which is correct.

So the code should work.

For a mask with m available elements (which is even), the number of possible pairs is (m choose 2) = m*(m-1)/2. For m=14, that's 91 pairs. For each mask, the code tries all possible pairs and takes the maximum. So for each mask, O(k) where k is the number of pairs for that mask.

The total time complexity is O(2^(2n) * (2n choose 2 average per state)). For n=7, 2^14 * 14*13/2 is about 16384 * 91 ≈ 1.5 million operations. Which is acceptable for C++.

Space complexity is O(2^(2n)) for the memoization.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxScore(vector<int>& nums) {
        int n = nums.size() / 2;
        unordered_map<int, int> memo;
        function<int(int)> dp = [&](int mask) -> int {
            int full_mask = (1 << (2 * n)) - 1;
            if (mask == full_mask) return 0;
            auto it = memo.find(mask);
            if (it != memo.end()) return it->second;
            // count used elements
            int used = 0;
            for (int i = 0; i < 2 * n; ++i) {
                if (mask & (1 << i)) ++used;
            }
            int op = (used / 2) + 1;
            // get available elements
            vector<int> available;
            for (int i = 0; i < 2 * n; ++i) {
                if ((mask & (1 << i)) == 0) {
                    available.push_back(i);
                }
            }
            int max_score = 0;
            for (int i = 0; i < available.size(); ++i) {
                for (int j = i + 1; j < available.size(); ++j) {
                    int a = available[i];
                    int b = available[j];
                    int new_mask = mask | (1 << a) | (1 << b);
                    int current_gcd = gcd(nums[a], nums[b]);
                    int current_score = op * current_gcd + dp(new_mask);
                    max_score = max(max_score, current_score);
                }
            }
            memo[mask] = max_score;
            return max_score;
        };
        return dp(0);
    }
};

solution: ```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int maxScore(vector<int>& nums) {
        int n = nums.size() / 2;
        unordered_map<int, int> memo;
        function<int(int)> dp = [&](int mask) -> int {
            int full_mask = (1 << (2 * n)) - 1;
            if (mask == full_mask) return 0;
            auto it = memo.find(mask);
            if (it != memo.end()) return it->second;
            int used = 0;
            for (int i = 0; i < 2 * n; ++i) {
                if (mask & (1 << i)) ++used;
            }
            int op = (used / 2) + 1;
            vector<int> available;
            for (int i = 0; i < 2 * n; ++i) {
                if ((mask & (1 << i)) == 0) {
                    available.push_back(i);
                }
            }
            int max_score = 0;
            for (int i = 0; i < available.size(); ++i) {
                for (int j = i + 1; j < available.size(); ++j) {
                    int a = available[i];
                    int b = available[j];
                    int new_mask = mask | (1 << a) | (1 << b);
                    int current_gcd = gcd(nums[a], nums[b]);
                    int current_score = op * current_gcd + dp(new_mask);
                    max_score = max(max_score, current_score);
                }
            }
            memo[mask] = max_score;
            return max_score;
        };
        return dp(0);
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(2^(2n) * n^2)
space complexity: O(2^(2n))
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses memoized backtracking with bitmasking to explore all valid pairings. For each state (represented by a bitmask), it calculates the maximum score by trying all possible pairs of available elements. The key strength is efficient pruning via memoization and bitmask state representation, which is feasible due to the small constraint (n ≤ 7). The main weakness is exponential time complexity, but it's acceptable for the given input size.
